HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS SENTINEL INSURANCE COMPANY LTD, THE HARTFORD FINANCIAL SERVICES GROUP INC., AND JEFFREY KAISER's Motion To Compel Deposition.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: THE COURT'S CALENDAR REFLECTS A FILING OF A NOTICE OF SETTLEMENT OF ENTIRE CASE (CONDITIONAL). THIS MAY HAVE MOOTED DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO COMPEL. THE COURT WILL ASSUME THAT THE MOTION HAS BEEN WITHDRAWN.

Plaintiff 1830 17th Street Llc'S Motion To Quash.

RULING - A R G U E D. Plaintiff argues re: email, request to continue for additional discovery. Defendant responds, argues alter ego, Plaintiff did not meet the burden. The Court denies Plaintiff's request to continue. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

SPECIALLY APPEARING DEFENDANT THE HARTFORD FINANCIAL SERVICES GROUP INC.'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Hartford Financial Services Group, Inc.'s ("HFSG) motion to quash is granted.

The parties agreed that HFSG could respond to the original complaint by July 24, 2019. Meanwhile, the parties met and conferred regarding dismissing "The Hartford" and HFSG and the filing of an amended complaint. Based on the parties' meet and confer efforts and the contemplated service of an amended complaint, it was not entirely clear when HFSG should have responded. (Austin Dec., Ex. 5, pg. 2 ["Unless plaintiff is willing to agree to dismiss HFSG now, what seems to make the most sense at this point is for plaintiff to go ahead and file the amended complaint dismissing The Hartford, and then we can continue to meet and confer as to HFSG based on all of our correspondence thus far."].) Based on these circumstances, the court finds that there is good cause to entertain the motion to quash even if it were technically filed late. (See Code Civ. Proc., 418.10(a).)

(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-01-2019 Overruled
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Demian Oksendler, Esq. 415-733-1050 of Mannion Lowe & Oksenendler for Plaintiff.
Tyler Austin, Esq. 650-637-9100 of Hayes Scott Bonino Ellingson Guslani Simonson & Clause LLP for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT JEFFREY KAISER'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Jeffrey Kaiser's demurrer to the cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is overruled.

In Bock v. Hansen (2014) 225 Cal.App.4th 215, the court held that "a cause of action for negligent misrepresentation can lie against an insurance adjuster." (Id. at 231.) Bock reasoned that an insurance adjuster is in a "special relationship" with the insureds, and that its conclusion is consistent with California law, which generally recognizes a cause of action for negligent misrepresentation in two circumstances: where providing false information poses a risk of and results in physical harm to person or property; and where information is conveyed in a commercial setting for a business purpose. (Id. at 229.)

Bock parted company with prior California case authority holding that "[a]n independent adjuster engaged by an insurer owes no duty of care to the claimant insured, with whom the adjuster has no contract. The adjuster is not liable in tort to the insured for alleged negligent claims handling which causes only economic loss." (Sanchez v. Lindsey Morden Claims Servs., Inc. (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 249, 250-251 [affirming order sustaining demurrer without leave to amend].) As the Sanchez court explained at length, there are good reasons for this general rule: "The insurer, not the adjuster, has the ultimate power to grant and deny coverage, and to pay the claim, delay it, or deny it. . . . [] An adjuster owes a duty to the insurer who engaged him. A new duty to the insured would conflict with that duty, and interfere with its faithful performance." (Id. at 253.) Further, the conclusion that no such duty should be imposed on adjusters is consistent with the law of agency, where the general rule is that agents are not liable to third parties for economic loss; the costs of imposing such a duty of care would outweigh the potential benefits; and doing so would significantly depart from existing California law and from the majority rule in other states, "which hold that an independent adjuster hired by the insurer owes no duty of care to the insured." (Id. at 253-256.) In the Court's view, Sanchez is the better-reasoned authority. However, Bock purported to distinguish Sanchez, and is binding on this Court. (See Auto Equity Sales, Inc. v. Superior Court (1962) 57 Cal.2d 450, 455.) Defendant's further argument that plaintiff has not adequately alleged a cause of action for negligent misrepresentation also fails in light of Bock. (See 225 Cal.App.4th at 231-232 [holding that insureds who alleged that adjuster falsely told them that their policy did not cover the cost of cleanup adequately alleged claim, and rejecting arguments that insureds did not justifiably rely on misrepresentation and did not sustain any damage].)

In addition, Defendant has 20 days to answer. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)
